fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

SIMPSON J:

INTRODUCTION

These are parenting proceedings between the Applicant father, Mr Livingston (“the father”), and the Respondent mother, Ms Tapia (“the mother”), in relation to the care of their only child, X (“the child”), born in 2021 and now four years of age.
The mother wishes to return to live permanently with the child in Country E, where she was born and lived until 2014, at which time she visited Australia on a student visa. The mother has remained living in Australia since that date, save for periods of travel and absences for visa purposes.
The father opposes the child living with the mother outside of Australia.

BACKGROUND

The mother (38 years old) and the father (36 years old) met in early 2019 in Australia and commenced their relationship and cohabitation shortly thereafter.
The father is employed in a managerial role and works full time. The mother, prior to the birth of the child, worked in a variety of roles, including as an educator. Following the birth of the child, the mother has had some limited part time or casual work only.
The mother was granted permanent residency in Australia in 2021.
The parties separated on a final basis in October 2022 when the child was one year old. The father left the former family home in December 2022 after finding separate accommodation.
The parties attended a mediation in January 2023 and reached agreement about the father caring for the child on three occasions each week, including all day each Saturday.
The father commenced these proceedings on 28 February 2023. Orders were made on 19 April 2023 placing the child’s details on the Family Law Watch List and specific orders were made for the child to travel with the mother to Country E, between dates in May and August of that year, in addition to directions for an interim hearing.
On 23 May 2023, orders were made by consent for the parties to hold equal shared parental responsibility for the child and for the child to live with his mother. Specific spend time orders were also agreed about Christmas and other special occasions. Those orders also provided for the sharing of information relating to the child and a range of other matters scaffolding the ongoing care of the child, including about changeover. An Independent Children’s Lawyer (“ICL”) was also appointed.
The matter was set down for interim hearing in September 2023 (after the mother would have returned from her trip to Country E with the child).
On 12 September 2023, further orders were made, after an interim hearing. The spend time arrangements for the child with the father changed to include overnight care, each Monday from after day care until the commencement of day care the next day. The father continued to care for the child for two hours each Thursday and for all of Saturday each week.
On 19 January 2024, the Family Report dated 18 January 2024 (“the Family Report”) and prepared by Ms F (“the Family Report writer”), was released to the parties.
In circumstances set out in further detail below, in April 2024, the mother unilaterally suspended the child’s time with the father. The spend time arrangements did not re-commence for more than seven weeks, until after the father had filed a Contravention Application against the mother. At a Case Management Hearing on 7 June 2024, the mother agreed to the resumption of the spend time arrangements as previously ordered. The father’s Contravention Application was heard on 20 November 2024 and the mother admitted three of the thirteen counts before the court (and that she did not have a reasonable excuse for the admitted contraventions). The parties jointly submitted to the court that no penalty or sanction should be imposed upon the mother.
On 26 February 2025, the matter was transferred to this Court given the international relocation sought by the mother and the likely length of the hearing.
At the time of final hearing, the child was continuing to spend time with the father in accordance with the orders of 12 September 2023 and had been experiencing one night of overnight time with the father each week, for more than 18 months.

THE DISPUTE

The father’s case

The father’s case outline document (and which became Exhibit 2) contained the orders he sought on a final basis. That document foreshadowed two sets of orders depending upon where the child was living – the arrangements which would apply in the event the child was living in Australia, and the circumstances which would apply in the event the child lived with the mother in Country E. While proffering an alternate set of orders to apply in the event the child lived in Country E, the father’s case was conducted on the basis that he maintained it was in the child’s best interests that he live with the mother, in Australia, and that the child’s time with the father progressively increase to a week about shared arrangement.
The alternative orders proposed by the father suggested a delay in the mother’s relocation to Country E with the child, until the child was due to commence school in 2027. Thereafter, the father proposed that he would spend time with the child in Country E and after the child turned 12 years of age, would also spend time with the child in Australia.
While the father did not assert that he could not live in Country E (if the court determined the child should live there with the mother) he maintained that it would be very difficult for him to do so. In opposing the mother’s orders sought to live in Country E with the child, the father asserted that he was likely to struggle to obtain employment in his field in that country, that he has limited Country E language skills, and that he may not be able to secure a visa to remain in that country for an extended period. As to the latter point, there was little evidence before the court to assist in assessing the ease with which the father could obtain the necessary visa to live in Country E or remain there for extended periods. The mother did not seek orders to apply in the circumstance of the father living in Country E.
The father also asserted that it was unlikely that his family would have the financial means to travel to Country E to spend time with the child and he identified a risk that the child would lose his relationship with the paternal family in the event the child lived in Country E.
The father more generally asserted a range of factors which he said weighed against the child living with the mother in Country E, including the adverse consequences for the child of spending less time with the father and the risk that the mother would not support and sustain the child’s relationship with the father and his extended family, in the event of a relocation.

The mother’s case

The mother had filed an Amended Response to Initiating Application on 11 April 2025, and a minute of final orders sought was part of her case outline document (Exhibit 4). The final orders sought on the mother’s behalf in both documents were the same and set out the orders the mother proposed in support of her primary application, that the child would live with her in Country E and the orders she proposed in the alternative, in the event she remained living in Australia with the child.
In the event the mother and child lived in Country E, the mother proposed orders that the child have “unsupervised overnight time with the father” in Country E and in Australia, not less than twice a year, as agreed and then within a specified framework. The default framework for spend time orders proposed by the mother included two occasions of time of not less than two weeks’ duration (in Country E and in Australia). In the event the mother and the child remained living in Australia, the mother proposed that the child would spend daytime periods with the father only, until the child turned nine years of age.
It became evident during cross examination of the mother that, contrary to the way her final orders sought were expressed, she did not in fact support the father having “not less than” two week periods of care of the child and in fact suggested that it was necessary for some “phasing in” of the father’s care of the child to occur, in the event she was living in Country E. Leave was granted to the mother’s counsel to obtain the mother’s further instructions (while the mother was still the subject of cross examination) and a further minute of orders sought by the mother was the product of those further discussions. The mother’s final amended minute of orders sought became Exhibit 41.
If orders were made providing for the mother live with the child in Country E, the mother proposed that the father care for the child for two periods of two weeks each year, one in Country E and the other in Australia. For the first year, the mother proposed that the father’s time with the child across a two-week period would include some overnight periods, followed by daytime periods. In a 14-day period, the father would care for the child for seven nights. The mother did not change the orders she proposed for the father’s care of the child in the event the mother and the child remained living in Australia – maintaining that until the child turned nine years of age, the father would care for him during daytime periods only.
While it is recognised that a parent who wishes to live in another place with a child does not have to establish compelling reasons for the proposed move, the mother sets out the bases for her desire to return to live in Country E. There is no suggestion that the mother’s wish to live in Country E is not bona fide and is based primarily on her desire to be reunited with her extended family and to receive the benefits of their support. The mother identifies financial advantages available to her in Country E (including access to accommodation at no cost to her), family support in caring for the child to enable the mother to more readily pursue employment, and greater employment prospects in that country.
The mother also asserts that she experiences a number of vulnerabilities in living in Australia, including financial pressures related to limited employment opportunities, the high costs of living she experiences in Sydney and the absence of the support and assistance of her parents and family members in Country E. The mother also asserts that she has some mental health vulnerabilities as a product of her unhappiness in continuing to live in Australia.

The Independent Children’s Lawyer

The ICL’s case outline document filed 7 July 2025 contained two proposed minutes of orders sought – one for in the event that the child remained living in Australia, and the other in the event that the child live with the mother in Country E. The ICL expressed a preliminary view that favoured the child remaining living in Australia, primarily in the mother’s care, but with an increase to the father’s care of the child, including block time in school holiday periods. The ICL generally reserved their position as to the orders to be made in the event the child lived in Country E with the mother.
At the conclusion of the evidence, the ICL supported orders being made that would see the child remain living in Australia, with his mother, and that the father’s time with the child would gradually increase.

The issues in dispute

Each of the parties filed a document setting out the issues in dispute (with each list of issues becoming separate exhibits, the father’s becoming Exhibit 3, the mother’s Exhibit 5, and the ICL’s Exhibit 7).
Leaving aside differences in phrasing, there was substantial overlap in the issues identified by the parties and the ICL which required determination. Each framed the primary question about the living arrangements for the child as being about the mother having “permission to relocate”. The use of such language has been discouraged by the High Court in AMS & AIF (1999) 199 CLR 160 (“AMS & AIF”) and subsequently by the Full Court (for example in Wagstaff & Wagstaff [2022] FedCFamC1A 119; (2022) FLC 94-098). As Kirby J observed in AMS & AIF:
[188] ... it would be preferable that such reference to “permission” to relocate be avoided. The word has a tendency to distract attention from the jurisdiction actually being exercised...To treat the determination of the residence of the child, and the connected issue of custody, as dependent upon the giving or withholding “permission” to a parent to relocate his or her residence may divert attention from the child's welfare, to the competing needs and demands of the parents in conflict.
As such, the court’s task is to identify and consider each of the parties’ proposals for the care of the child, assessing the advantages and disadvantage of each proposal for the child and to determine which arrangement is in his best interests.
The father opposes the mother’s application that the child live with her in Country E. It is not disputed that the mother will not leave Australia without the child. On that basis, the father does not seek orders that the child should live with him in Australia. Consequently, the court is required to determine which of the following scenarios (as proposed by the parties) is in the best interests of the child:
(a) To live with the mother in Country E (as she seeks) and the spend time arrangements between the child and the father, which ought to apply:
(i) in the event the father lived in Country E, or

(ii) in the event the father continued to live in Australia.

(b) Or, whether it is in the best interests of the child for him to live with the mother in Australia (as the father seeks but contrary to the preferences of the mother) and the spend time and communication arrangements between the father and the child in that event.

In identifying the above issues for determination, I recognise that neither the father nor mother expressly sought an order about the father living in Country E. The court is not bound to only consider the proposals expressed by the parties (U & U [2002] HCA 36; (2002) 211 CLR 238 (“U & U”)) and may explore a range of outcomes which may be in the best interests of the child, subject to procedural fairness considerations being met. The father was cross examined about the possibility of him living and working in Country E. While identifying difficulties and impediments to him doing so, the father did not rule out the possibility of living in Country E if the child was based there. It is proper that such an option still be assessed.
In considering the live with options summarised above, consideration will also occur as to the extent to which the mother is able to support the child’s relationship with the father. The father contends that the mother does not value nor properly support the child’s relationship with him and that such would be even more imperilled in the event the child lived with the mother in Country E.
Similarly, consideration of the primary living arrangements for the child will also include assessment of the impediments (if any) to the mother remaining living in Australia (as she asserts) and whether the mother’s mental health and general wellbeing (and by implication, her parenting of the child) will be enhanced if she was living in Country E (and conversely, the consequences for the mother and upon the child, in the event the mother is compelled to remain living in this country).
The father asserts that he understands the importance of the support that the mother derives from her extended family in Country E and proposes that the mother would continue to have the opportunity to spend time with the child in Country E, for extended periods, consistent with the travel arrangements in place before the child was born and agreed between the parties in the years following his birth.
The mother seeks to place limits upon the time that the child spends with the father. She seeks to explain those limitations based on her observations and beliefs about the child’s behaviour before and after he spends time with the father and her views of the father’s failure to properly provide for the child’s needs when in his care. The mother asserts that the father continues to present some risk to the child such that she says his care of the child in Australia should not include overnight periods until the child is nine years of age.
The evidentiary basis for that position included the mother’s assertions that the father had engaged in family violence to her and that the child had been exposed to conduct by the father which had resulted in injury to the child (which, at its highest, the mother appeared to suggest was the product of neglect rather than deliberate action by the father). Notwithstanding these propositions, the mother sought orders that the father’s care of the child occur without supervision and inconsistently, if the time occurred while she lived in Country E, the time would include overnight periods. These tensions and inconsistencies were not adequately explained by the mother in her evidence nor in final submissions on her behalf.
The legislation requires the court to make orders that are in the best interests of the child and which promote the child’s safety and the safety of those people responsible for the care of the child. This requires the court to look to the future to identify the arrangements which are determined to be in the best interests of the child. Consideration of, and fact findings about, historical allegations are necessary only to the extent to which such findings inform the assessment of prospective risks of harm for the child (Pickford & Pickford [2024] FedCFamC1A 249 (“Pickford”) at [79]-[87]).
The mother asserts that she experienced family violence from the father during the relationship and that the child was exposed to family violence. In part, it appears that the mother relies upon those historical allegations to found her continuing opposition to the child having overnight time with the father (if the child remains living in Australia) and to have limited overnight time in the event the child lives in Country E (coupled with her other allegations as to neglect or inadequate care of the child by the father). This is notwithstanding that the child has been having overnight time with his father since September 2023 (save for the seven-week suspension of time imposed by the mother) and the support given to the continuation (and extension of) this arrangement as expressed by the Family Report writer.
The mother also asserts that she experienced aspects of the father’s engagement with her to have coerced and, or, controlled her (including as to financial control). The parties are in dispute about long-term decision making for the child. Consideration of these matters may inform the nature of the parties’ parenting relationship and the prospects of them meaningfully engaging in consultation in aid of long-term decision making in the best interests of the child.
While mindful of the caution expressed by Austin and Williams JJ in Pickford at [87] – that trial judges should only resolve those factual disputes between the parties “which authentically influence the outcome” – I will consider and determine the mother’s allegations as to her experiences of family violence and other contentions which she says create some risk for the child in the father’s care. This is notwithstanding her orders sought embracing unsupervised time between the child and the father, given these contentions, identified above, may have also impacted the parenting relationship between the parties.
The parties were also in dispute about the extent to which they had agreed to live in Country E in the future (as the mother contended and the father disputed) and about whether the father had told the mother to leave Australia and return to live in Country E with the child (as the mother asserted and which was at least to some extent disputed by the father).
The father asserted that the mother had long held a preference to live in Australia and had not intended to return to live primarily in Country E (which was disputed by the mother).
None of these contentions require determination by the court in circumstances where the resolution of those disputes does not materially assist in the assessment of what parenting orders will be in the best interests of this child now. The evidence makes clear that during the happier phases of the parties’ relationship, they may well have discussed the possibility of living in Country E in the future (including when they were travelling in that country together), while contemplating scenarios that included them enjoying a long and mutually satisfying relationship. Equally, the mother, while in an intact and happy relationship with the father, may have acted in a manner consistent with someone wishing to remain living in this country.
Similarly, at the time of the breakdown of the relationship and when conflict between the parties was high, it is possible that the father made representations to the mother like those she has asserted (to the effect that he would not oppose her moving back to Country E with the child).
The consequence of the birth of the child, the end of the parties’ relationship, and the process relating to, and the implementation of, the care arrangements in place for the child in the three years following separation, provides a proper foundation for each party to maintain that they have changed their mind from positions expressed previously. The birth of the child and then the separation between the parties are major intervening events which may have influenced either parent reassessing their preferences and priorities. Even if a finding was to be made about one version of these contentious historical matters over another, that determination does not advance resolution of the question of what parenting orders are in the best interests of this child at this time.

MATERIAL RELIED UPON

The father relies upon:
(a) Further Amended Initiating Application filed 14 February 2025;

(b) His affidavit filed 2 June 2025;

(c) Affidavit of Ms G filed 2 June 2025; and

(d) Affidavit of Ms H filed 2 June 2025.

The mother relies upon:
(a) Amended Response to Initiating Application filed 11 April 2025;

(b) Her affidavit filed 16 June 2025;

(c) Affidavit of Ms J filed 24 February 2025;

(d) Affidavit of Mr K filed 25 February 2025; and

(e) Notice of child abuse, family violence or risk filed 17 May 2025.

Each of the parties relied upon the Family Report which became Exhibit 8.
In addition to cross examination of each of the parties and the Family Report writer, the father’s sister was also cross examined.
A range of documents were admitted into evidence and made exhibits in these proceedings. I have had regard to that material. I set out below an evaluation of the analysis of the various proposals of the parties, in the context of an evaluation of the evidence overall. I have listened carefully to, and observed closely, each of the parties and witnesses being cross examined. If I do not refer to a particular issue or document in these reasons, that does not mean that it has been overlooked or not taken into account by me.

THE FAMILY REPORT

The Family Report writer met the parties in late 2023 (both through digital means and in person) and observed the child with each of the parents on 7 November 2023. Before her cross examination, the Family Report writer was provided with the parties’ trial affidavits, the affidavits prepared by extended family members, and the documents which had been tendered and made exhibits at that stage.
The Family Report writer identified a limitation to her assessment as a result of the mother’s reliance upon an interpreter for her interview and during the observations with the child (Exhibit 8 at page 3). Primarily, the Family Report writer identified that the interpreter appeared “limited in their capacity to understand the complexity of family violence, and the mother’s reports of such” and identified that such may have impacted upon the Family Report writer’s ability to obtain a clear understanding of the mother’s allegations about such. In addition, the interpreter is noted as not translating the communication between the mother and the child (who only had limited English language skills at the time of the interview and communicated with the mother in Country E language) during their observation. The Family Report writer indicated reliance upon her observations of body language and other cues when assessing the relationship between the child and the mother.
No cross examination of the Family Report writer about these identified limitations occurred and no submissions were made about those factors impacting the opinions of or recommendations made by the Family Report writer.
While the observations underpinning the Family Report were made almost two years prior to the final hearing, neither party nor the ICL submitted that the delay impacted the efficacy or utility of the opinions expressed in the report. There was no challenge to the expertise or experience of the Family Report writer.
The Family Report writer’s oral evidence expanded upon, and amplified matters expressed in her written report. I was significantly assisted by the evidence of the Family Report writer, which I accept.
The Family Report writer observed of the child that he was a bright and happy child who appeared to be meeting his expected developmental milestones (Exhibit 8 at [78]). The Family Report writer commented in Exhibit 8 as to the consequences for the child in the event he was to live with his mother in Country E and expressed a number of recommendations in the Family Report. Those recommendations were premised on the basis that the court would determine the primary issue in dispute, being whether the child lived with the mother in Country E or remained with the mother in Australia.
The Family Report writer noted that at two years of age (as the child was at the time of observation) a child can tolerate longer separations from his parents and if a child exhibited some distress in response, such was not automatically reflective of the quality of the parenting the child received nor did it necessarily mean that the child did not wish to spend time with the other parent (Exhibit 8 at [78]). The Family Report writer opined that the child’s ability to manage this change and transition would be enhanced by parents providing “patient, consistent, loving and supportive care” and for changeovers to be “free of family violence and/or parental hostility, arguing and tension” (Exhibit 8 at [78]).
The benefits to the child of living with the mother in Country E were identified by the Family Report writer and included (Exhibit 8 at [82]):
(a) Giving the mother access to “family support and counselling in her native language”;

(b) As supporting the mother’s wellbeing, described as “vitally important” for the child given she is his primary caregiver and he is “highly reliant upon her to meet his needs and is likely to be attuned to her when she is not coping”;

(c) As supporting the mother’s improved financial situation and benefits flowing to the child from such, derived from the mother’s asserted improved employment prospects in that country and reduced reliance upon external support for childcare; and

(d) The flow on effect of the asserted improved financial circumstances, reducing the financial pressure on the mother and the potential that such impacts upon the mother’s mental health, with potential adverse consequences for her parenting of the child.

However, the Family Report writer also identified “potential implications” for the child if a relocation occurred, including the impact upon his capacity to remain connected to the father and the extended paternal family. The Family Report writer expressed reservations as to the utility of video communication as a means of maintaining the child’s connection to the father (Exhibit 8 at [83]). It was also noted that given the mother only speaks Country E to the child when in her care, a move to Country E may result in the child being “disconnected” from his Australian culture (Exhibit 8 at [84]). The Family Report writer observed that connection to the child’s Country E culture could be fostered by spending significant time with his mother in Country E, each year (Exhibit 8 at [85]).
While acknowledging the mother’s expressed concerns as to the father’s parenting capacity, the Family Report writer observed (Exhibit 8 at [83]) that:
... there does not appear to be any clear indication, or collateral information available, that would indicate [the father] is unable to meet [the child’s] fundamental care needs ... [the child] has formed a meaningful relationship with [the father], with a similar bond to that he has with his mother.
Of the observations of the child with each parent, the Family Report writer commented:
[Mr Livingston] interacted with [X] in a similar educative manner as the mother, though supported [X] to explore the room on his own for longer periods without recalling him. [X] appeared to enjoy the independence, however returned to his father frequently with toys to play with. [Mr Livingston] followed [X’s] lead in play, only interjecting when he noticed an opportunity to teach [X] a new word/concept. Though [Mr Livingston] engaged with [X] with less intensity and narration than that of [Ms Tapia], [X] appeared to equally enjoy the time he spent with his father, displaying smiles in the same manner as during his observation with his mother. [X] remained intensely focused on [Mr Livingston] as he spoke and appeared intrigued by his father's play, mimicking [Mr Livingston] the best he could.
[X] appeared equally content in his father’s presence as during the observation with his mother. There was no observable difference in terms of [X’s] proximity with the parents, with him choosing to remain close with each of them unless he was exploring the room. The only marked difference appeared to be the languages each parent spoke with [X], the level of parental stimulation, and the extent of [X’s] exploration the parents supported.
The following recommendations were expressed:
If [X] remains in Australia, the parties share parental responsibility. If [X] relocates to [Country E], [Ms Tapia] have sole parental responsibility for [X], though ensure [Mr Livingston] is kept apprised of her decision making.
If [X] remains living in Australia, that he continues to live with [Ms Tapia] and spend time with [Mr Livingston] in accordance with the current arrangements. That [X] increases his time with his father to two consecutive overnights from his third birthday, and there is a gradual increase of overnights, with at least three months in between increases, after [X’s] fourth birthday, until he is living week-about with each parent.
If [X] is permitted to relocate to [Country E], that he communicates with his father via video call twice per week at a time that is suitable to [X’s] routines. That [Ms Tapia] travels with [X] once per year to Australia for at least two weeks, and any other time as agreed between the parents, with [Mr Livingston] responsible for the cost of [X’s] flights, and [Ms Tapia] the remaining cost of the travel. That [Mr Livingston] is permitted to travel to [Country E] to spend time with [X] on at least one occasion per year, for at least two weeks, and he is responsible for the cost of his travel. That regardless of which parent facilitates the travel, throughout the two-week block, [X] spends overnight time with his father in a progressive manner that is in accordance with the recommendations above [in para. 97], though this does not limit his day visits with [Mr Livingston].
Considering the allegations of family violence, the parents only communicate via written means (i.e., text messages, parenting application, email), and ensure all changeovers occur either at [X’s] educational facility, or at a public place to mitigate the risk of his further exposure to harmful behaviours by either parent.
That both parents provide summary feedback to the other regarding their time with [X], including changes to the child's routines (i.e., sleep, dietary), how he has coped with increases in time away from his mother, and if [X] injures himself. That the parent's communication is limited to sharing this information and requesting additional information required to care for [X] (i.e., medication), and is not utilised to criticise the parenting skills of the other parent.
That neither parent is under the influence of illicit substances nor consumes alcohol over the legal limit to drive whilst responsible for [X].
That [Mr Livingston] obtain a written report, and provide this to the Court, from the psychologist he reportedly attended upon for a mental health assessment, detailing any assessments completed by the clinician and the results of such, and clinical recommendations for treatment and/or ongoing support if relevant.
That for any future negotiations required, including changes to [X’s] time spending with his father, either parent’s travel arrangements overseas, and/or other matters that would be considered significant decision making, the parents seek the assistance of either a private mediator or the Family Relationship Centre.
In her oral evidence, the Family Report writer stated about the primary issue of relocation:
And while it’s up for the court to determine if he should relocate, I would have significant concerns if he were to relocate - that perhaps wasn't as strongly portrayed in my report.

(Transcript 18 July 2025, p.479 lines 30–32)
As to the mother’s proposal to cease overnight time until the child turns nine, the Family Report writer observed:
Well, I would think, given that the child is nearly four, he would be able to cope with more overnight time rather than less. I’m – I’m a bit, I guess, perplexed as to why less is being offered, and I'm not sure whether information is coming out throughout the course of the proceedings of, like, whereabout. But that does confuse me, and I imagine it would be quite confusing for the child himself as well to spend less overnight time with - with Dad.

(Transcript 18 July 2025, p.480 lines 15–21)
In response to questions about the nature and quality of the father’s relationship with the child at this age, the Family Report writer said:
He is in that age, [X], where he is still forming his foundational relationships. So he needs as much time as possible with his father to form and maintain that relationship and to really cement that for him as a - a - a - a secondary or major relationship aside from his mother. When there are significant gaps - gaps in the - in the time that they spend together - but just keeping in mind little people, in particular, they're very concrete thinkers and they also have memories insofar as they remember what has recently happened. It’s very hard for them to draw on long memories like an older child might do. So he would definitely be forming that relationship with his dad. He would be forming these memories each time that he would go to see him, which would be reinforced each time he spends time with Dad, particularly if there's some routine, which it certainly seems like there is - some routines in what Dad does with him. That then gives [X] some consistency and something that he knows is going to happen, which then helps him to be settled in that time that he spends with Dad and away from his primary caregiver, which is Mum. So it's incredibly important that that time continues because until he reaches the age of late childhood, early - early adolescence, he will rely on that continued time and that continued relationship to form those long - long memories with Dad. If he were to relocate, for example, he might spend time with Dad. It might be for a substantial period - once or twice a year. But there would almost be a bit of a re-introduction each time he would come back and spend time with Dad because if they're quite a significant period of time where he's not with Dad, little people are not just - they don't just get a sense of comfort at a time but also things like smell, so what Dad smells like, what his house smells like, what sort of food Dad cooks. They all help to cement that familiarity for him and - and that comfortability spending time with Dad. So if he were to relocate, [X] would lose all of that and then he would have - he would have to rebuild that each time he starts to spend time with Dad.

(Transcript 18 July 2025, p.487 lines 2–28)
When asked about the potential impact on the child of exposure to the mother in a vulnerable state, including financial vulnerability, the Family Report writer opined:
Well, I would imagine at - at the very least he - he would be experiencing his mother perhaps in a heightened - a heightened and constantly stressed state, which then might impact upon her - despite her best efforts, to be as attuned to him as what she would normally be and provide him with that really quality caregiving that she does strive to give him. And so we know that children feel that and they become quite restless. They don't have the verbal skills to articulate that. So it starts to become a - in their behaviour, whether it's towards other adults or peers. If it’s a prolonged issue and she is experiencing prolonged and - stress, by nature, then I would imagine when he starts school, he would probably have difficulty - not disconnecting from her but saying goodbye to her in the morning. There would be that, I guess, insecure - insecurity in him to be able to leave Mum’s side because children become quite attuned to their parents’ stress and by nature, tend to want to stay by that parent and a bit of - bit of assurance. So he might have difficulties detaching from Mum at school and engaging in school work. We know that children who suffer prolonged stress can have things - issues with their ..... they can regress in behaviours. It can affect their diet. It can affect the - particularly for young children, the formation of their brain because we know their brain is rapidly growing, particularly in the first five years. And they don't do well - or they don't deal well with stress. But they - they have so much growth going on that they ..... to everything around them. So the - if they are - if they endure prolonged stress, then that becomes their - I guess, almost their response. So they can ..... heightened stress state, which then can, in turn, again, impact behaviour. They almost can remain stuck in a ..... response. So they have difficulty engaging with peers in the playground and with teachers, and they have difficulty focusing on education and they have - in learning routines, difficulty transitioning between homes. There’s just a raft of potential implications for him.

(Transcript 18 July 2025, p.493 lines 22–48)
In response to questions about the mother’s observations of the child’s behaviour after time with the father, the Family Report writer observed:
I think where I was starting to lead into was [X’s] experience of both his parents at that point in time, and I - I guess his sense of their anxieties, because it's not uncommon for parents, particularly parents in constant - what I would call - say is high conflict, to be quite anxious when they're either talking about the other parent or heading to changeovers. It comes to quite a - a - an onerous and stressful experience for the parents, and that then undoubtedly filters down to particularly little children, because they feel that. So it wouldn't surprise me if he was perhaps particularly quite disruptive when he returns. But I would like to point out he is still quite little, and it's not abnormal for children to, particularly when they're spending quite - reasonably short periods of time with a parent, so that that time tends to be on most occasions packed full of activities and things that they may not do with the other parent, and there’s excitement that goes with spending time with their non-resident parent – that’s not abnormal behaviour. I can't speak to the vomiting. I can't speak to anything - might be diet-related. That’s probably best asked through a GP. But I would say that changes in behaviour are not uncommon, which is why that consistency in time and routine is so important and that - and parents to really be on their best behaviour when it comes to communicating with each other, facilitating changeovers, how they spoke to their - the child about the other parent. All those things are really important, because they build the child’s experience about their time with each parent.

(Transcript 18 July 2025, p.495 lines 21–40)
The Family Report writer opined that the behaviours of the child observed by the mother could also indicate that the child was experiencing some anxiety and stress in relation to the co-parenting relationship (Transcript 18 July 2025, p.495 line 47).
In response to questions from me about “best practice” of preparation and assistance for the child in the event a relocation was to occur, prior to that relocation, the Family Report writer replied:
I think the challenge is that he would - even if he were to spend a significant portion of time with his father and paternal family before he leaves, the challenge is that – is maintained in that ongoing connection. And then if he weren’t to have frequent time with his father, which I imagine is not possible given the distance to [Country E] and I imagine the cost, there – there is – there’s undoubtedly going to be a significant rupture in that relationship. So I think it would be important for – for the child to spend as much time as possible with his father before he were to leave, but I don’t know that that would serve as a protective factor to maintain – to maintain and nurture that relationship until he’s older.

(Transcript 18 July 2025, p.502 line 46 to p.503 line 7).
In response to a question about the impact on the child of having no overnight time (as sought by the mother, if the child remains in Australia, until the child turns nine years of age) the Family Report Writer opined:
Well, I think for [X], in particular, he would be quite confused, I imagine. But he – as I said much earlier on, he seems to have already become accustomed to spending overnights with Dad, and that would have required a lot of work on Dad’s part to get [X] into some routine where he can actually manage to spend nights away from Mum. So he started to shift from – he still has his primary caregiver, which is incredibly important, but he has now got a reliable secondary caregiver who he goes to to spend that time with. So there’s a lot of confusion for little children because he would not understand why that time is reduced, what the – the purpose of it. And then he wouldn’t understand – and even if he gets older, he wouldn’t understand why he can’t spend, I guess, more time with Dad that he would want to spend with him. So I think from a developmental perspective, that’s difficult to answer in terms of the overnights. It would be – the – the – I guess the – the quantity of time overall that I would be more concerned about. But I’m really not sure as to why nine seems to be this idea of when [X] can cope with overnights when he’s managing that now.

(Transcript 18 July 2025, p.504 lines 7–21)
The Family Report writer observed that if a child does not speak of the other parent, or ask questions about them, that silence does not necessarily suggest that the relationship between the child and the parent is not important or established (Transcript 18 July 2025, p.504 line 39).
The Family Report writer added:
I think it’s also important to point out that even very little children know when they can’t speak about another parent without it causing angst. So they do become very – they’re very clever at working out, I guess, the information they can seek from a parent. So even if he were to be missing Dad and he had the verbal skills to say that he was, I would be surprised if he did say that to – to Mum, given the relationship between his parents.

LEGAL FRAMEWORK

These proceedings are to be determined with reference to the Part VII of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”). The objects of that Part, set out in s 60B, are:
(a) to ensure that the best interests of children are met, including by ensuring their safety; and

(b) to give effect to the Convention on the Rights of the Child done at New York on 20 November 1989.

The child’s best interests are the paramount consideration as made clear by s 60CA.
When determining what is in the child’s best interests, the court is directed to have regard to the following considerations set out in s 60CC(2) of the Act:
(a) what arrangements would promote the safety (including safety from being subjected to, or exposed to, family violence, abuse, neglect, or other harm) of:
(i) the child; and

(ii) each person who has care of the child (whether or not a person has parental responsibility for the child);

(b) any views expressed by the child;

(c) the developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs of the child;

(d) the capacity of each person who has or is proposed to have parental responsibility for the child to provide for the child’s developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs;

(e) the benefit to the child of being able to have a relationship with the child’s parents, and other people who are significant to the child, where it is safe to do so;

(f) anything else that is relevant to the particular circumstances of the child.

So called relocation cases are not a special category of parenting cases (Morgan & Miles [2007] FamCA 1230; (2007) FLC 93-343). They are to be decided through the application of the same statutory framework as all parenting cases and the orders made are to reflect the best interests of the child. It is not a question of assessing the proposal of the parent who wishes to relocate against the “status quo” but instead, the court must identify and evaluate each of the proposals under consideration (Sayer & Radcliffe [2012] FamCAFC 209; (2012) 48 Fam LR 298 at [47]- [48]).
The court is not bound by the proposals made by each party in determining what care arrangement is in the best interests of the child (U & U) but must afford natural justice to the parties to the extent a novel outcome is under consideration.
The court is obliged to explore the mother’s desire to live in Country E, not to determine if she has “compelling reasons” to relocate (which High Court authority, U & U for example, makes clear the mother is not required to demonstrate) but to explore the manifestation of her right to freedom of movement and what that might mean for the child. The statutory obligation to consider the best interests of the child as the paramount consideration does not mean the court will ignore the legitimate interests of a parent to live where they choose. However, where there is conflict between these considerations, priority is to be given to the best interests of the child, as explained in AMS & AIF:
[144] ... a statutory instruction to treat the welfare or best interests of the child as the paramount consideration does not oblige a court, making the decision, to ignore the legitimate interests and desires of the parents. If there is conflict between these considerations, priority must be accorded to the child’s welfare and rights. However, the latter cannot be viewed in the abstract, separate from the circumstances of the parent with whom the child resides.
That assessment will accordingly require the court to look carefully at the various scenarios for the care of the child under consideration. The court must explore alternatives to restricting freedom of movement, especially where it is proposed to impose upon the primary caregiver the obligation to provide the care of the child in a place not of their choosing (Sampson & Hartnett (No 10) (2007) FLC 93-350).
The Full Court in Franklyn & Franklyn [2019] FamCAFC 256 gave more recent expression to that principle as follows:
There is an inherent tension between, on the one hand, separated parents being able to establish new homes wherever they like and, on the other, their restraint by injunction from living too far apart to avoid any impingement of their children’s ability to retain meaningful relationships with both parents. The conflict is between the best interests of the children to know and have regular personal contact with each parent and the interests of the parents to enjoy a high measure of freedom of movement which is not lost by reason only of their parental responsibility for the children (see AMS v AIF (1999) 199 CLR 160 (“AMS v AIF”) at 196, 206, 207-208, 210). The tension at the intersection of those conflicting interests is even greater when an order is sought, not just to restrain one parent’s move further away, but to compel the parent who has already moved away to return and establish a new residence closer to the other parent.
While the children’s interests are paramount, their interests are not the sole determinant of parenting orders under Part VII of the Act (AMS v AIF at 207, 225, 230; U v U [2002] HCA 36; (2002) 211 CLR 238 (“U v U”) at 282). Parents enjoy as much freedom to live where they please as is compatible with their obligations pertaining to the children (see AMS v AIF at 223-224, 231-232; Sampson and Hartnett (No.10) [2007] FamCA 1365; (2007) FLC 93-350; Zanda & Zanda [2014] FamCAFC 173; (2014) FLC 93-607 at [132]- [136]). Only when the children’s welfare would be adversely affected must a parent’s right to freedom of mobility defer to the paramount consideration of the children’s best interests (see U v U at 262).

CONSIDERATION

Safety considerations

Section 60CC(2)(a), as set out previously in these reasons, is informed by s 60CC(2A) of the Act, which provides as follows:
(2A) In considering the matters set out in paragraph (2)(a), the court must include consideration of:
(a) any history of family violence, abuse or neglect involving the child or a person caring for the child (whether or not the person had parental responsibility for the child); and

(b) any family violence order that applies or has applied to the child or a member of the child’s family.

Family violence is defined in s 4AB(1) of the Act as follows:
For the purposes of this Act, family violence means violent, threatening or other behaviour by a person that coerces or controls a member of the person’s family (the family member), or causes the family member to be fearful.
Section 4AB(2) of the Act provides a non-exhaustive list of examples of behaviours that may constitute family violence, including:
(a) an assault; or

(b) a sexual assault or other sexually abusive behaviour; or

(c) stalking; or

(d) repeated derogatory taunts; or

(e) intentionally damaging or destroying property; or

(f) intentionally causing death or injury to an animal; or

(g) economic or financial abuse; or

(i) preventing the family member from making or keeping connections with his or her family, friends or culture; or

(j) unlawfully depriving the family member, or any member of the family member’s family, of his or her liberty.

The mother asserts that the father has engaged in family violence towards her and that the child has been exposed to aspects of that conduct.
I note that no ADVO has been made in this matter at any time.

Alleged teddy bear incident

The mother alleges that when the child was very young, around one year old (in October 2022), she observed the father to hold a teddy bear on top of the child’s face and that he persisted in holding the toy in that fashion, despite the mother’s comments.
The mother agreed in cross examination that (to her observation) the child was not distressed, though she says his face was red (when she removed the teddy bear) but she was not suggesting that the child’s breathing had been impaired at the time she intervened. The mother said in her oral evidence that she was uncertain if the father intended to harm the child in these circumstances. While the mother suggested in her oral evidence that she (sometime later) referred this incident to the police, there is no record of such a report in the police documents produced to the court.
The father denied the event as described by the mother and avowed that he had no memory of such an incident and that he was sickened upon reading the allegation as expressed by the mother in her affidavit. The father was clear in his rejection of this event both in his written and oral evidence and there was nothing about the manner in which he gave his evidence which caused me concern as to the veracity of what he said.
The state of the evidence is such that I am unable to find that any such incident occurred, as alleged by the mother.

Pram incident

The mother alleges that on an occasion in January of 2023, when the father came to the home to collect the child, an incident occurred outside the home when she says the father deliberately pushed the pram into her legs; that he threw a paver/block at the parties’ dog and that he spoke to her angrily, using abusive language. The father denied the use of abusive language or acting angrily on that occasion. The father said that the pram hit the mother’s foot accidentally, and that he moved the paver (which propped the back gate in place) with his foot and the movement of the block startled their dog. He denied that the block/paver became airborne and denied that the paver/block struck the dog.
The mother spoke to the police in April 2023 and referred to these (then historical) events. The police subsequently spoke with the father. The father gave an account to the police which is consistent with his written evidence about these events. The account given by the mother (as recorded in the police document) differed somewhat from her written and oral evidence.
The police document records that the mother advised them that she had made a video after the events set out above. That video was not provided by the mother to the police or to the father (despite his request for it) and is not in evidence.
I refer to Exhibit 33 being an application for an Apprehended Domestic Violence Order (“ADVO”) made by the mother in March 2023 in which the pram incident is referenced. The creation of that document is closer in time to the date on which the alleged incident is said to have occurred. In that application, the mother again gave an account of this incident which was different in some parts to that given in her written evidence. In that document, it is reported of this incident, that the child was being held by the father (as he contends in his evidence). The mother maintained in cross examination that the father was not holding the child, that he deliberately pushed the pram into her legs and that he threw the block/paver at the dog.
I prefer the father’s evidence about this event. He remained consistent in his account of what had occurred, and his evidence was not changed as a result of cross examination. In contrast, the mother has given slightly different accounts of this event to the police and when seeking an ADVO as compared to her written evidence. The mother delayed in reporting her concerns about this incident to the police and seeking an ADVO. Her delay was unexplained and does not support her contention that she believed that this was a deliberate act by the father and that it made her fearful of him.
I cannot find, as the mother alleges, that the father deliberately pushed the pram into the mother’s legs nor that he deliberately flicked (or threw, as the mother alleges) the paver/block at the dog.

Asserted violent and controlling behaviour by the father to the mother

The mother contends that she experienced the father to be controlling of their finances and on occasion, refused to provide her with funds sufficient to meet her needs and those of the child, at times when she was largely dependent upon the father for her financial support.
Particular examples were provided in the mother’s written evidence, and the father was cross examined about these contentions. The father denied unreasonably withholding funds from the mother but accepted that there were occasions when the mother wanted money to buy additional food items and he refused, based on his belief that there was sufficient food and other provisions at home available to the family. The father’s oral evidence was to the effect that he was concerned about the family living within their means and trying to keep within a budget, following the birth of the child.
The father was not moved in cross examination about these events and his explanation for his response to the mother’s requests was plausible. I consider the context in which these events have occurred. The evidence tends more to the conclusion that the parties at times experienced conflict about their finances in the relationship, especially as they adjusted to their changed circumstances following the birth of the child. I am not satisfied that these events constitute financial control by the father of the mother.
The father accepted that he made certain comments to the mother, following the end of the relationship but when they still lived together, critical of her for seeking a child support assessment against him and that at that time, he was upset that she had taken these steps and he considered her approach to be “unfair”.
The father explained, and I accept his evidence, that he was frustrated that the mother took these steps at a time when he was already meeting the costs of their household, including rent and the costs of household supplies and services (and when the mother was not working outside the home, given she was caring for the child). In the context of the conflict and pressure of a recent separation and parties living under one roof, I accept that communication between the parties may have become more inflamed and difficult and that the father communicated to the mother his frustration and unhappiness with her decision to seek child support.
In this context, the father also sought to explain why it was that he removed the mother’s access to a car which he had bought (subject to finance) for the family’s use. The father accepted that he had access to a work vehicle. His decision to remove the family car to prevent the mother’s use of it reflects poorly upon him. In a message to the mother sent on 31 December 2022 (Exhibit 19), in response to her message about the impact on the child of not having the car, the father said:
You should have thought about this before fighting me on everything and being so disrespectful. The childcare is around the corner, above Coles. You can walk there. And for everything else, there is public transport, or you will need to buy your own car.
Later, the father agreed that he had taken the car after the mother had taken steps to obtain an ADVO against him and in a text he sent to her at that time, he said there was “no forgiving you” and that his actions were retaliatory.
I find that the father’s actions in removing the car from the mother were punitive and inconsiderate, and I find that he took these steps because he was displeased with the mother’s engagement with him. The father’s actions at this time were poorly considered by him and suggestive of immaturity in his dealings with the mother. I accept that these events would have added to the mother’s confusion and vulnerability and have no doubt fuelled distrust between the parties.
Similarly, the mother asserts that the father was regularly critical of her and that he used rude and demeaning language towards her when they argued. The father accepted in his oral evidence that he had used certain abusive and obscene language in reference to the mother though he rejected use of certain of the terms put to him. The father accepted that he, at times, called the mother “cunt” (when they were arguing) and in his oral evidence, he expressed shame and remorse about that conduct. In response to questioning from me about when he realised his behaviour was shameful, the father said he felt immediate regret and on occasions, apologised. However, even on the father’s account, that poor conduct occurred on more than one occasion, despite his asserted shame and remorse about his conduct.
I am satisfied that the father did on occasion use abusive language towards the mother during the relationship and around the time of their separation. That conduct is unacceptable and I am satisfied that such conduct is family violence in accordance with the legislative definition. I recognise that the mother’s experience of the father’s conduct towards her has impacted upon her trust of the father and the co-parenting dynamic between them, as discussed later in these reasons.

The throwing of a dining chair

The mother alleges that during another instance of conflict between the parties, the father became angry and threw a dining chair (in March 2021). The father disputes that this occurred as alleged by the mother and in his oral evidence, admitted to knocking a chair over during an argument with the mother. The father contended that the chair fell backwards when he struck it with his hand, that he righted it immediately and returned it to sit underneath the table.
The mother accepted in her oral evidence that she did not call the police at the time and that she did not refer to this event at any later point in subsequent discussions with the police. The mother also accepted that when she sought an ADVO in March 2023, she also did not cite this event as part of the background provided in that application. It is improbable that the mother would not have cited the father’s actions as part of the background to her ADVO application if it had occurred in the manner she describes in her written evidence.
I am not persuaded that the father threw the chair in the fashion alleged by the mother, but I accept even on the father’s account that this would still have been a startling and potentially frightening event for the mother.

The father coming into the mother’s home uninvited

The mother has also set out evidence of occasions when she says the father has behaved inappropriately at her home, when there to collect or return the child. The father denied the conduct attributed to him by the mother.
The father had retained a key to the home and the mother asserted that there were occasions when he entered the home without her agreement (which was denied by the father). It was not controversial that there were occasions when the father came to the home, with the child, and entered with the mother’s agreement, both when she was home and on occasions when she was not. The mother alleges that on 31 December 2022, the father came to her home in the early morning, uninvited, used the spare key to enter the property and began screaming at the mother for making a child support application.
The father denied that anything like the mother suggested had occurred. It was his evidence that the parties (and his friends and sister) spent time together that day for New Years Eve (which was shortly after the father had moved out of the home).
A range of text messages were exchanged between the parties that day (Exhibit 19). The messages illustrate the ongoing conflict between the parties, and they were arguing about a series of issues that day. Each complained about the conduct of the other over historical matters. The messages do not directly corroborate the mother’s allegations as to the father’s conduct that morning although she does assert that he yells at her in front of the child on occasions when at the home (among other things). There is no suggestion in the messages that the yelling had occurred that morning, nor in the circumstances as alleged by the mother.
Given the parties’ very different accounts of these events, and that neither was moved in cross examination, the evidence is such that I am unable to determine whether the events occurred as the mother has alleged. However, the evidence of both of the parties and Exhibit 19 makes clear that they frequently argued and these events occurred shortly after their separation when the conflict between them was high.
The events addressed above, which featured significantly in the cross examination of the parties, occurred more than three years ago. I take into account that the parties have separated and that there have not been more recent instances of such conflict between them. The making of orders limiting the parties’ engagement with each other (as is sought by each party) will be protective of the mother.

Documents completed on behalf of the mother or with her input

These events summarised above, provide a backdrop to steps taken by the mother in the post-separation period to secure supports and services, including through a local women’s support service, seeking assistance from Victims Support. The mother was cross examined about the contents of a document completed on her behalf to obtain government funded or subsidised services (Exhibit 31). An account of the mother’s alleged experience of family violence was included in that document, including the assertion that family violence had been experienced by the mother from early 2019 (effectively from the commencement of the relationship). That assertion is not consistent with the mother’s written evidence. Other discrepancies between the detail in that document and the mother’s written evidence were explored in cross examination.
The mother distanced herself from the inconsistencies between her written evidence and the contents of that document by asserting that she had not directly completed the document; the mother was reluctant to accept that the document reflected an accurate account of the information she had provided to the worker assisting her (the person who completed the document). However, the mother conceded that she had provided information to her social worker and that the topics she discussed with the social worker were covered in that document, though using language perhaps different to the words she had used.
English is not the mother’s first language. I accept her evidence that it is possible that there was a disconnect between her and the person with whom she worked about things she attempted to communicate and some of the detail expressed in that document on the mother’s behalf.
The mother asserted that the same difficulty explained differences between her written evidence and the content of other documents which had been completed on her behalf, but ostensibly based on her instructions, including when the mother applied for an ADVO against the father in March 2023. That application was Exhibit 33.
The mother agreed, and I accept, that the NSW Police had refused to apply for an ADVO on behalf of the mother against the father and instead, in March 2023, she sought to make her own application. In that process, the mother was assisted by a person at the court (whom she believed was a lawyer). After that application was lodged and a copy of the document was sent to the mother, she replied to the court advising of a number of matters she wanted to correct to her application. Those communications became Exhibit 34.
I accept that the mother identified errors in her application only after it had been filed and that she took some steps to remedy those errors in her email communication to the court. The mother did not formally amend her application for an ADVO.
Again, given the language challenges the mother experiences, I do not accept that the inconsistencies identified in these documents (against what the mother has said in her written evidence) should found a criticism of the mother’s truthfulness or credibility. It is not at all difficult to comprehend that confusion and misunderstanding may arise between two parties communicating when each of them do not share a primary language, especially about complex and nuanced matters such as alleged family violence.
I also note that the mother was not successful in her application (the father attended the first court date and indicated that he opposed the orders sought) for an interim ADVO and she discontinued her application for the ADVO the day before the matter was listed for hearing in July 2023.
In April 2024, the mother unilaterally suspended the child’s time with the father, despite interim orders being in place. I have regard to Exhibit 36 which are the documents produced by NSW Police which include records of their communications with the mother in May 2024 when she spoke with them about her concerns for the child in the father’s care at that time. That document makes clear that the mother advised the police that she was seeking to change the orders about the child’s time with the father, but she did not advise the police of the nature of her “concerns” about those arrangements.
The police documents record the mother’s reports about the father continuing to attend the shopping centre where changeovers usually occurred. The police later speak to the father, clearly because of the mother’s reports, about his attendance at the shopping centre. It is recorded in that document that the father confirmed that he was continuing to attend changeover on the days and times that he was supposed to collect the child, in accordance with the orders.
The mother rejected in cross examination that she had told the police that the father was still attending the shopping centre despite the suspension of his time. To explain the inclusion of that information in the police document, the mother suggested that the police were “making it up”. The mother denied that she had attended the changeover location on the occasions that she had suspended time and had no explanation for the inclusion of the information about changeover in the police documents, other than invention by the police.
The police document includes the report that “the victim believes that [the father] is ‘stalking’ her”. The mother rejected that she had said such a thing to the police. The police document also notes the following, among other things:
(a) Despite the mother believing that the father was ‘stalking’ her, “it appears that [the father] is only attending the locations where he is supposed to meet to see his son.”

(b) “It appears that [the father] is continuing to attend the day care and meeting points outlined in the current court orders, to be able to see his son. However, at present the victim is preventing this from happening.”

(c) “During interactions the victim stated to Police that [the father] has made no threat towards herself or their son and she is not scared for their safety.”

In her oral evidence, the mother did not accept that she told the police that she was not fearful of the father and denied that she had attended the shopping centre to observe the father there, waiting for delivery of the child. Despite the mother’s contentions to the contrary, I find that the mother made the reports and comments attributed to her by the police in that document. The mother’s denials about these matters were unconvincing. The mother is the only plausible source of this very specific information expressed in the police record.
It was submitted on behalf of the father that the mother had displayed a persistent willingness to blame others for inconsistencies between documents, prepared on her instructions, and some of her evidence in these proceedings. While I have recognised and make allowance for the difficulties the mother has experienced in communicating in English with some of the service providers, that challenge does not fully explain the position the mother took in her oral evidence when confronted by some of these inconsistencies. The mother’s evidence that the police made up parts of the contents of Exhibit 36 has been rejected by me. Her evidence in this instance causes me some concern about her truthfulness and goes beyond difficulties in comprehension and communication for a non-English speaker.
In any event, these difficult events occurred more than three years ago. The parties have not lived together since December 2022. On each party’s proposed orders, their physical engagement with each other will be limited to only some occasions of changeover. I am satisfied that the fact that the parties no longer live together and that their need to have regular face to face engagement with each other can be minimised, means that the parties (and the child) will be shielded from the risk of the repeat of these types of events. I note the opinion of the Family Report writer about the benefit to the child of him experiencing his parents engaging in a courteous fashion with each other. These parties are capable of managing themselves in this fashion and I am satisfied that the risk of conflict between the parties will be adequately managed by appropriate changeover arrangements being made.

The views of the child

At the time of the final hearing (which concluded in August 2025) the child was four years old. Neither party submitted that consideration of the views of the child was appropriate, given his age.

The needs of the child

The Family Report writer observed that the child appeared to be meeting his age-appropriate developmental milestones (based on observations undertaken when the child was two years old). At the time of the hearing, neither party contended that the child was experiencing any challenges in meeting relevant milestones or was experiencing other vulnerabilities.
The child was identified by the Family Report writer to benefit from a close and established connection to both parents.
In her oral evidence, the Family Report writer observed that the child at this age and stage of development, was:
... still forming his foundational relationships. So he needs as much time as possible with his father to form and maintain that relationship and to really cement that for him as a ... secondary or major relationship aside from his mother.

(Transcript 18 July 2025, p.487 lines 3–6)

It’s very hard for them to draw on long memories like an older child might do. So he would definitely be forming that relationship with his dad. He would be forming these memories each time that he would go to see him, which would be reinforced each time he spends time with Dad, particularly if there’s some routine, which it certainly seems like there is – some routines in what Dad does with him. That then gives (the child) some consistency and something that he knows is going to happen, which then helps him to be settled in that time that he spends with Dad and away from his primary caregiver, which is Mum. So it’s incredibly important that that time continues because until he reaches the age of late childhood, early – early adolescence, he will rely on that continued time and that continued relationship to form those long – long memories with Dad.

(Transcript 18 July 2025, p.487 lines 9–19)
The evidence readily establishes, and I find, that the child is much loved and well cared for in both households, despite the mother’s remaining uncertainty about the father’s capacity to meet the child’s needs, discussed more fully later in these reasons.
The child is Australian, with Country E heritage on the mother’s side and Australian heritage on the father’ side. On each parent’s orders sought, the child will be encouraged and supported to maintain his exposure to, and connection with, his cultural heritage.
I explore these matters more fully below when considering the capacity of the parents to meet these identified needs of the child.

The capacity of each parent to provide for the child’s needs

The child was a little over one year old at the time of separation. The mother provided more of the early care of the child prior to separation and in the immediate aftermath, given that the father continued in his employment outside of the home. After the parties lived in separate locations, the evidence makes clear that the father sought to have regular and consistent time caring for the child. It is to the credit of the parties that they were initially able to agree to those arrangements between them.
Since orders were made in April 2023, the father has shown himself to be interested in the child and progressing his relationship with the child. The father has exhibited an understanding of the benefit to the child in gradually increasing the time he cares for the child (as expressed to the Family Report writer in Exhibit 8 at [62]) and as reflected in his orders sought. I accept that evidence as suggestive of the father’s increasing understanding of, and sensitivity to, the needs of the child.
I accept the evidence of the Family Report writer, and I find that the child has benefitted from and has a sound and positive relationship with both of his parents. However, as is explored further below, the Family Report writer identified that the child’s relationship with the father is still developing.
The mother asserts deficiencies in the father’s capacity to provide for the needs of the child. The mother identifies behaviours by the child after he has been in the father’s care which she says (in part) founds her contention that the continuation of overnight time is not in the best interests of the child. The mother maintained that contention in her trial affidavit, notwithstanding the views of the Family Report writer in the Family Report dated 18 January 2024, to the effect that such observed behavioural responses or “changes” are not necessarily indicative of a deficiency in care being provided to the child by the other parent (as set out previously in these reasons).
The mother maintained, including in her oral evidence, a belief that the father was not providing the child with adequate or appropriate food nor ensuring the child was sufficiently hydrated while in the father’s care. The mother has maintained those criticisms of the father’s care of the child, despite the father providing the mother with quite detailed summary reports of the child’s experiences when with him (which daily summaries became Exhibit 46).
The mother would not express support for the competence of the father’s parenting of the child in her oral evidence and repeatedly relied upon the observation that, as she was not present, she “did not know”. Despite this, later in her oral evidence, the mother was prepared to express a view of the father’s parenting style. She did not agree that such a style might be described as “a bit more relaxed” than her own, instead saying his style was “Very easily to be – to get angry, to get irritated” (Transcript 17 July 2025, p.318 lines 1–2).
While she accepted that the child was close to his father, the mother did not accept that the daily summaries provided by the father provided reassurance about the competence of his parenting.
The mother maintained that she did not see benefit in the father being involved in long-term decision making with her as in her view, he did not add much to the process and “escalated” matters when he was involved.
In response to questions from the ICL, the mother maintained that she continued to believe that the child was at risk of being “neglected or abused” in the father’s care and that she believed that the child’s needs were not being met by the father when in his care. In her oral evidence, the mother maintained her view that the child’s dietary and nutritional needs were not being met by the father; that the child continued to be tired after overnights with the father (which she took to mean the father’s reports about the child sleeping well were not truthful); and that generally he was at risk in the father’s care.
