When the mother was asked to explain how she would support the child in moving to Country E, and the absence of his father, the mother’s answers were tangential and non-responsive, instead focussing on the strength of the child’s relationship with the maternal grandmother and extended family and the nature and frequency of his communication with them.
It is quite clear that the parties have different approaches to the parenting of the child and the father (not disrespectfully) described the mother as being very attentive and more like “a helicopter parent” (by which I took him to mean a parent who is vigilant and watchful of the child’s activities and actions at all times, and in its more critical usage, as if “hovering” over the child).
The father has been caring for the child three times a week, including overnight on Monday evenings, since 12 September 2023. In addition to the evidence of the Family Report writer as to the child’s strong relationship with the father, and the father’s own evidence about the care he provides to the child, I also have regard to the unchallenged evidence of the paternal grandmother and the father’s sister about their observations of the father’s care and parenting of the child. I accept their evidence as to the loving and appropriate care provided by the father to the child on those occasions observed by each of them.
I am satisfied and find that the father is a capable and loving parent who is attentive to the child’s daily routine and is able to provide well for his day-to-day needs. I do not accept the criticisms of such made by the mother, which are inconsistent with the weight of the evidence.
The mother also asserts more serious allegations about the father’s care of the child (notwithstanding her case outline maintaining that this was not a case about unacceptable risk). The mother maintains that the child has experienced unexplained bruising and marks to his body, that he has been sunburned, and that the father has not been sufficiently attentive to, and protective of, the child while in his care.
On 15 April 2024, the mother instructed her lawyers to write to the father’s lawyers to advise him of her intention to suspend the child’s time with the father (Exhibit 27). That letter alleges the child experiencing “injuries and behavioural issues” after spending time with the father. The child is described as acting in a manner which suggested he was “distressed, confused, tired and aggressive.” In cross examination, the mother would not accept that some of the behaviours she described might be explained because of the age of the child, his busy life, and the impact of transition between, and differences in, the two households.
The mother’s unilateral suspension of the father’s court ordered time with the child continued for more than seven weeks. The mother admitted that despite the concerns expressed in the April letter, she had not shared those concerns with the police prior to that letter being sent.
In April 2024, the mother took the child to be reviewed at L Hospital, she says, because she saw the child behaving “strangely”. In her written evidence, the mother asserted that she observed bruises on the child’s body “which were smaller on that day than his usual but still noticeable”. In her written evidence, the mother asserted that the doctor who examined the child advised the mother that they would report the matter to “child protection”.
The mother later provided a series of photographs of the child to the hospital.
It is not controversial that the NSW Department of Communities and Justice (“DCJ”) did not take any action after these inquiries.
In the seven weeks that the child did not spend time with the father, the mother suggested that the child’s routine improved and that this time was “beneficial” for the child.
The mother maintained in her oral evidence that the child did not mention the father and did not appear to miss him. The Family Report writer opined that such was not necessarily indicative of a child who was not missing his father but might suggest that the child was attuned to the mother’s attitude in relation to and about the father.
When being questioned about her revised proposal, that in the event she remained living in Australia, the child would not have overnights with his father until he was nine years old, the mother maintained that “no overnights for him right now is actually – could be a positive” for the child.
The mother maintained in cross examination that the injuries and bruises she had observed on the child were not consistent with the sorts of marks an active toddler might experience; similarly, she did not accept that his observed tiredness might be the product of a busy toddler who was usually engaged in very active play. Instead, inferentially, the mother maintained that what she observed was the product of some failing or neglect on the part of the father.
The mother did not advise the father of the child’s hospital attendance in April 2024, despite an express obligation in the orders of 23 May 2023 that she do so. The father only found out about the child’s attendance at hospital when he was contacted by police later in April 2024.
The father commenced contravention proceedings on 10 May 2024 and that application was heard on 20 November 2024. The mother agreed to the father’s time with the child resuming prior to the hearing of the contravention application.
On 10 June 2024, the child returned to the father’s care. The father asserts that the child was hurt that afternoon when they were playing at home (in what might be described as ‘rough and tumble play’ on the bed at the father’s home). The father was concerned for the child and took him to hospital to be reviewed. He contacted the mother and advised her of these events such that she then joined him at the hospital and was present when the doctor cleared the child of injury and he was released. The child returned to the father’s care after the hospital visit (in accordance with the orders).
The mother deposes to the father speaking in a threatening fashion to her while they both waited for the child. This was denied by the father and he was unshaken in cross examination about this. The state of the evidence is such that I do not find that the father spoke with the mother in the way she contends.
Despite the mother’s suspicions, the evidence does not permit a finding that the child’s time with the father is the “cause” of any negative factors or changes in behaviour observed by the mother. I do not accept and do not find that there is any proper basis to conclude that the child has suffered bruising or unexplained injury in the father’s care, beyond that which might be expected of an active and happy child, engaging in experiences in the wider world consistent with his developmental needs and progress.
I recognise that the parties may adopt different approaches to parenting. Those general differences, without more, does not mean that one approach is better or worse than the other. I am satisfied that the child is well cared for in both households.
As is expanded upon elsewhere in these reasons, the mother’s lack of trust in the father and her willingness to disregard information contrary to her thesis about the father, is a feature of the parenting dynamic between the parties. The implications of the mother’s very poor attitude to the father’s parenting capacities are expanded upon later in these reasons.

Matters impacting the mother’s capacity to care for the child

The mother wants to return to live in her country of origin and expresses the desire to derive the support and care of her extended family available to her in that country. The mother asserts that she is unhappy living in Sydney and that she feels alone and unsafe around the father.
Cumulatively, it is suggested that the mother is living in less than optimal circumstances in this country, given financial pressures (which include asserted limited employment opportunities; reliance upon subsidies with respect to her rent which the mother says may not be available to her after the middle of February 2026 and after which her rent would increase and become unaffordable to her); her separation from her primary family supports; and her asserted vulnerable mental health, about which she has sought assistance from a number of sources since separation.
The mother contends that these pressures, summarised above, impact upon her ability to provide optimally for the child and contends, in accordance with the well-recognised aphorism, “happy mother, happy child” – that the child’s wellbeing will be enhanced if he lives with the mother in Country E.

The mother’s mental health vulnerabilities

The mother has attended upon a psychologist since around early 2023 and reported seeing her psychologist on a weekly basis. No report from the psychologist was provided, though two letters from the psychologist dated 16 May 2024 and 28 March 2023 became Exhibit 51 and 52 respectively. Those letters were admitted on the limited basis as confirmation of the dates upon which the mother had attended with her psychologist and the contents of the letters were not otherwise read.
I accept and find that the mother has identified benefits to be derived from ongoing psychological support and she has been diligent and proactive in accessing services to receive this assistance since early 2023. However, I have no evidence as to the nature of the psychological support being provided to the mother, nor is there evidence before the court as to any presently diagnosed mental health condition experienced by her.
It was submitted on behalf of the mother that the mother’s “mental health” would be improved in the event she lived in Country E and had access not only to her family support but to mental health services in her own language. Given the absence of evidence of any underlying mental health condition, I am unable to accept that submission.
The state of the evidence means that it is not open for me to conclude that the mother is experiencing mental health impairment in her current living circumstances, nor that she will experience the improvement of such in Country E compared to Australia. Similarly, I am unable to find that an order requiring the mother to remain in this country is likely to result in her experiencing impaired mental health functioning (which, it was submitted, would be adverse to the best interests of the child).
I am satisfied that the mother is genuine in her stated preference and desire to live with her family in her country of origin and that she says she feels alone and with little support in this country. I accept that the mother would receive additional, practical support if she lived in Country E, and that she has experienced practical challenges in being a single parent, alone in this country. The mother has however, continued to meet her obligations to the child in an admirable fashion, despite the challenges she has identified, while living in this country. The evidence establishes that the mother has been able to provide excellent care to the child, despite what she says have been her experiences and the evidence does not suggest that her parenting of the child has been compromised.

Cultural needs

The cultural heritage associated with the country in which the child lives is likely to be more readily experienced by the child. Each of the final orders sought by the parties will impact upon the child’s ability to experience his cultural heritage fully and each has advantages and disadvantages.
In the event the child lived with the mother in Country E, there is no doubt that he would be able to engage more comprehensively in developing his understanding of his Country E heritage, building upon his experiences obtained during previous extended holidays in that country and the regular (daily) engagement he has with the maternal family (via digital means) while he has lived in Australia. The child speaks Country E language in his home with the mother and has attended Country E language child-care centres. The father speaks a combination of Country E and English with the child, but the father describes his Country E language skills as being “limited”.
The father identifies that if the child lives with the mother in Country E, it is likely that his English language skills will reduce and the Family Report writer identifies some risk that the child will lose his connection to his Australian heritage as a consequence of such a change. I accept that evidence as even with exposure to bi-lingual education in Country E, which the mother supports, the practical reality is that in his home life, the child will continue to speak Country E language (given the extended family members speak Country E language to him now and would be likely to communicate in that language with him predominantly).
In the event that the father was also able to live in Country E, his regular engagement in the care of the child would go some way to ensure the child’s connection to his Australian heritage was maintained.
I have regard to the affidavit of the paternal grandmother who was not required for cross examination. I accept her unchallenged evidence that, given her age and financial circumstances, travel to spend time with the child in Country E is unlikely to occur.
Similarly, the father’s sister deposed in her written evidence to her desire to remain involved in the life of the child and wishing to support the father in his care of, and relationship with, the child. The father’s sister identified that she was unlikely to be able to travel to Country E as her husband’s family were from another country and that location would consequentially be the focus of their international travel. The father’s sister was cross examined but no change to her evidence was elicited as a result of that process. I accept her evidence.
In the event the child remained living in Australia, it is likely that his identification with his Australian cultural heritage will be strengthened. The child will however continue to live with the mother in that scenario and will also have, on that basis, continued daily exposure to the Country E language and culture (through his mother) at home and in the continuation of his regular communication with his maternal grandparents and other family members based in Country E.
In the event the child remained living in Australia, the father proposes that the mother and child would continue to travel to Country E to spend time with her family, as she has done in the past, for up to two months. Once the child starts schooling and thereafter, the father proposes the child travel for up to a month at a time.
The unchallenged evidence of the maternal grandmother was that the child was very familiar with her (because of their daily communication) and that he appeared to remember her home and his bedroom there when he visits. This evidence, which I accept, supports the finding that the child’s connections to the maternal family and aspects of their lives in Country E is well established.
While the arrangements of the child travelling to Country E each year are not equivalent to the child living in Country E, I am satisfied that it will be possible for the child to continue to be able to experience his maternal cultural heritage even in the circumstances of living in Australia.

The benefit to the child of being able to have a relationship with each of his parents and other people who a significant to him, where it is safe to do so

Consistent with authority (including U v U at 285-286) it is generally accepted that except in relationships characterised by abuse, children benefit from the development of positive relationships with both of their parents.
The child’s central relationship with the mother, his primary carer, will be sustained on the orders sought by each of the parents. As identified by the Family Report writer, the child is dependent upon the mother to meet his needs as his primary carer. The mother is meeting the child’s needs very well, despite the circumstances in which the mother now finds herself in this country.
The father has consistently identified the importance of the child’s relationship with the mother and has readily acknowledged her parenting competence and that he does not hold any concerns about her ability to care for the child.
I have found that the child has a positive and safe relationship with the father, from which he derives many benefits. There are opportunities for the child’s relationship with his father to continue to develop, given he is only having one night of time with the father each week. The development of an additional primary relationship with the father was identified by the Family Report writer in her oral evidence (as set out in these reasons previously) as important for the child.
While it was submitted on the mother’s behalf that she understands the benefits to the child of his relationship with the father, and is supportive of such, those submissions are contradicted by a range of matters, including her decision to seek to remove overnight time (in the event the child lives in Australia) and her opposition to the progression of the child’s time with the father more generally. The mother’s continuing ambivalence about, and opposition to, progression of time was difficult to understand and in her oral evidence, the mother expressed continuing distrust of the father’s capacity to care for the child.
The mother applies a very high standard of care for the child, including careful focus upon nutrition. I infer from the mother’s evidence and her attitude to the father that she considers the care she provides to the child to be superior to that provided by the father. Even if differences in the parenting approaches of the parties were established, that in itself does not mean that the child is at risk in the father’s care, or that such risk is greater during overnight periods. There also remains the unresolved tension that at the same time the mother seeks to suspend overnight time in this country, the mother proposes that if the child lives in Country E, some overnight time with the father may occur.
The mother in her oral evidence was reluctant to concede a positive view of the father, for example, she was unprepared to recognise that he was operating as a “good parent” when he took steps to secure medical attention following the incident at his home in June 2024.
The mother’s attitude to the father’s relationship with the child is troubling and evidence of her concerns is not corroborated by independent sources.
There are no ancillary documents before the court which suggest the child is not thriving (for example, day care records or records from the child’s general practitioner).
The mother relied upon a range of photographs which she said revealed bruises or marks to the child’s body which were of concern to her and which she said were not adequately explained by the father (Exhibit 50). I am unable to discern from review of those coloured images anything remarkable. The father has sought to reassure the mother about his care of the child, including by providing “summaries” of their activities and his care; it appears however, that if the father does not have an answer for the mother about how a particular bruise or bump or mark has been caused to the child, the mother concludes such has been “caused” by the father or his neglect.
The mother also asserted of the video in evidence (Exhibit 26) that the child clearly spoke of his desire to return to the mother in that recording. That interpretation of the words said by the child, when in a very distressed state, was not agreed. The father’s evidence was that the child had become distressed because he had been told that it was time to leave the paternal family gathering and he did not want to leave. I am unable to determine what words the child said as such was unclear from the recording. Similarly, I am unable to determine what “caused” the young child to be distressed at that time, given there may have been a series of factors impacting such, including his very young age, the time of day, his level of tiredness and other matters unknown.
I recognise that this child is very precious to each of his parents. The father admitted to making the recording in Exhibit 26 in a desperate (and dishonest) attempt to persuade the mother that he was experiencing car problems and could not return the child at the expected time. This was in circumstances where the mother had agreed on only one other occasion to extend the child’s time with the father, when the child had fallen asleep adjacent to his return time.
The mother remains highly critical of the father’s care of the child. Her binary approach to cause and effect with respect to her observations of the child, soon after the child has been with the father, results in an impossible situation for the father – his inability to adequately explain what the mother sees leads the mother to conclude that something has happened to the child while in the father’s care and to be distrusting of the father. I do not share the mother’s view about these issues.
I am persuaded that the mother holds a very poor attitude to the father and his ability to care for the child which is not properly based. This poor attitude causes me grave concern as to her ability to protect, foster and support the child’s relationship with the father, in the event the child lived in Country E.

Relationships with other people significant to the child

The child has important relationships with his extended maternal and paternal families.
The evidence comfortably satisfies me that this child is loved by the members of his extended family in Australia and in Country E and has developing and positive relationships with them. Maintaining relationships with his grandparents and aunts and other family members is important for the child’s well-being and development.
Obviously, the opportunity to maintain and develop those relationships will be enhanced for the child with the family members living in the country in which the child lives. If the child remains living in Australia, the mother will travel to Country E with the child each year (on each of the parents’ orders sought) and I accept that the mother will continue to foster the child communicating regularly with his Country E family, as is occurring at this time.
In the event the child lives in Country E, the mother proposes orders which will facilitate the child being in Australia on one occasion each year and for communication between the father and the child. It is less clear the extent to which the paternal family will be able to maintain their relationships with the child in these circumstances, given the limited time the child will spend in this country and the possible language challenges identified elsewhere in these reasons.
In the event the father lives in Country E, the child will more readily maintain his relationship with his father and in that instance, the father would no doubt take steps to facilitate the child communicating regularly with family members based in Australia. The limited opportunities for those family members to travel to Country E has been noted elsewhere.

Other matters that are relevant to the particular circumstances of the child

The father is paying child support in accordance with an administrative assessment. There are no arrears. While the mother was critical of the father’s conduct shortly after separation with respect to financial issues, I am not satisfied that those events are reflective of a continuing disregard for meeting his financial obligations as a parent. I take into account that the father, through his solicitors, volunteered to make an additional payment to the mother, on an ongoing basis, in an attempt to assist her financially (Exhibit 44). The mother agreed in her oral evidence that she had not instructed her solicitors to respond to that proposal, and she observed that it was “not very much money”. While the latter observation may be accurate, the father’s actions suggest he has some insight into and acceptance of the difficulties the mother says she experiences in her current living circumstances and has offered some further financial assistance to the mother.

THE SCENARIOS UNDER CONSIDERATION

I turn now to consider the different care arrangements for the child which are under consideration.
In the event the chid was to live in Country E, with his mother, I am satisfied that most of his needs would be met in those circumstances. The child would continue to have the benefit of his mother’s excellent care, and the mother would be able to receive the additional support of her family, which she desires.
The mother identifies a preference to live in Country E, and I accept that in doing so, she would have access to the loving support of her family. The mother identifies practical advantages of living in Country E, including having access to family support and the prospect of sharing accommodation with her mother (and the financial advantages of such). The mother asserts that her ability to find employment in Country E will be greater and she will have ready childcare support through her family. I accept that evidence.
I accept the mother’s evidence, and I find that the mother is likely to experience some relief and greater contentment in returning to live in Country E with the child. As identified by the Family Report writer, and as accepted by me, the child is likely to derive some benefits in his primary caregiver being in an environment where she experiences greater family and social support and where she is within her own cultural framework.
In living in Country E, the child will also have greater access to his maternal family and to opportunities to enrich his exposure to his Country E heritage and culture. Those relationships and experiences are important to the child, and I find are already well established given the daily communication which the mother fosters between the child and the maternal grandmother (and others) in Country E, and as reflected in the close relationship which the maternal grandmother says exists between she and the child.
In the event the father also lived in Country E, the child would have the benefit of the continuation and development of the important relationship between the child and his father.
However, I am not satisfied that it is reasonably likely that the father could live in Country E. An order about this reciprocal living arrangement was not sought by either party and there was limited evidence about this scenario. The father deposes to having limited Country E language skills (having stopped formal lessons, but I accept he could resume his efforts to learn the language) and has no clear employment prospects in that country. The father suggested that his skills would not readily translate to employment opportunities in that country. I do not have the benefit of evidence about this, beyond the father’s assertions.
There was no evidence before the court about the visa requirements for foreign nationals living in Country E. The state of the evidence is such that uncertainty around this remains and I cannot safely infer that the father would in fact be successful in making such arrangements.
In these circumstances, I am not persuaded that it is appropriate to embark upon further speculation about the viability of the father living in Country E.
If the child lived in Country E and the father travelled there to spend time with him, such would be limited by the amount of leave the father could obtain (and again, I have limited evidence about the terms of the father’s employment) and, or, his ability to take time from his work without pay.
The mother proposes two periods of two weeks for the child to be in the father’s care each year, if she lives in Country E, with one occurring in Country E and one in Australia. It is likely on this scenario, that the child will only spend face to face time with the paternal family on one occasion each year, in Australia. That will mean that the child’s connection to, and relationship with, the wider paternal family is likely to remain more limited on this scenario and will not develop.
The paternal grandmother’s evidence, which I accept, was that she will not readily be able to travel to Country E to see the child. Similarly, I accept the evidence of the father’s sister that she is unlikely to be able to travel to Country E to spend time with the child as her family will prioritise overseas travel to spend time with her husband’s family.
Of greater concern, however, is the impact upon the child’s important relationship with his father, in the event the child lived in Country E with his mother and the father remained living in Australia. In that scenario, as a practical reality, the opportunity for the child to spend day to day, in person time with the father will be significantly reduced. I refer to, and accept, the evidence of the Family Report writer as to the benefits to the child of his relationship with the father, which she described as foundational, and the importance of facilitating the development and expansion of that relationship for the child as was made clear in her oral evidence.
The mother contends that regular digital communication between the father and the child will go some way to assisting the maintenance of the relationship between them.
The father contends that in the past, when the child has travelled to Country E with the mother, he has experienced difficulty in securing regular communication with the child. The evidence around those events remained unclear and the mother maintained that the father’s criticisms of her were unfounded and unfair. The experiences of the parties may have been impacted by the challenges of meeting the routine of a much younger child. In any event, time differences between the two countries add one non-controversial impediment to regular telephone or video communication.
The limitations of this type of contact were identified by the Family Report writer, and she opined that such was not a substitute for in person time, which remained important for the child in the creation and retention of memory and relationship building with the father. I accept that evidence.
In addition to the limited in person time the child would be able to spend with the father on this scenario, as stated previously, I hold grave reservations more generally as to the mother’s ability to foster and support the child’s relationship with the father. I have regard to the mother’s persistent highly critical views of the father’s ability to safely and appropriately care for the child, which I have found to be without foundation and contrary to the evidence but which she has maintained in the more than three years since separation.
The mother was prepared to unilaterally suspend the father’s time with the child, despite orders being in place, which suspension continued for more than seven weeks. It is uncontroversial that the mother did not resume time until after the father commenced contravention proceedings.
Notwithstanding the mother formally admitting to three breaches without a reasonable excuse (Exhibit 37) on 20 November 2024, at the final hearing, the mother expressed the view in her oral evidence, that the circumstances were such that her actions were justified. The fact that the mother persists in that view, is indicative of her fixed critical views of the father.
I also have regard to the mother’s failure to advise the father of her actions in taking the child to be reviewed by doctors at L Hospital in April 2024 (one of the breaches admitted by the mother in November 2024). The father did not become aware of these events until advised by the police later that month. The mother in her oral evidence was unempathetic to the father’s experience, in not being advised of his child’s hospital attendance. There was an order in place at the time, expressly obliging the mother to provide such information to the father. In her oral evidence about these events, the mother appeared to contend that given her belief that the father’s actions may have been the cause of the child’s perceived injuries, she did not need to tell him about the hospital attendance.
I find that the mother exhibited little insight into the impact of her choices upon the father and I was not persuaded that she was regretful about her past conduct.
Having endorsed a particular set of final orders from April 2025 to the commencement of the final hearing, the mother sought to vary her proposed minute on the fifth day of the final hearing. As identified previously, the mother’s revised position would see the suspension of overnight time between the child and his father until the child turned nine years of age, if the child lived in Australia. A different arrangement would apply in the event that the child lived in Country E, the child would have limited, phased in, overnight time with the father before progressing to block periods of 14 days, but still with limited overnight time
The inconsistency between the various scenarios proposed by the mother remained unresolved. The mother could not adequately explain why overnight time with his father was to be denied for this child for a further five years (in Australia) but with different arrangements applying if the child lived in Country E. The possible adverse impacts for the child of the removal of overnight time appeared not to be understood by the mother and she maintained her view that this would “be better” for the child.
The Family Report writer did not support the elimination of overnight time between the child and his father and opined that such would likely be confusing and destabilising for the child (who has enjoyed for more than two years a stable routine of weekly overnight time with the father).
The evidence persuades me that the mother does not properly value the relationship that the child has with the father. I hold grave concerns about the mother’s ability to properly support the child’s relationship with his father in the event the mother and child lived in Country E. The risk of the loss of the child’s still developing and important relationship with the father, weighs heavily against the mother and child living in Country E.
In considering orders requiring the mother to remain living in Australia with the child, I also have regard to the consequence of such for the mother. The mother, the child’s primary carer, does not want to live in this country. If her application to return to live in Country E is refused, I accept that she will be disappointed.
The mother has lived in this country for 11 years. She has permanent residency. I accept that the mother has been able to obtain some employment in the past in this country and she has experience in a number of fields which may provide her with opportunities for additional employment, once her responsibility for care of the child modifies – either as a consequence of the child’s engagement with formal schooling and, or, as a consequence of him spending more time with the father. The mother may pursue employment as a dental nurse, having the relevant qualification and experience.
I also take into account the range of services in this country which the mother has been able to access, in the post-separation period, including access to a case worker, ongoing counselling services and rental subsidies. I accept the mother’s evidence that she has had the benefit of a rental subsidy for the last three years which is due to expire on a date in February 2026. The mother’s eligibility for a range of services, through several providers, allows the inference, which I make, that the mother is likely to be eligible to access similar supports in the future.
The mother has opportunities to develop additional friendships and connections in the community in which she lives, through her employment and through the child’s attendance at school and engagement in extra-curricular activities, as he gets older.
I accept that the mother will continue to receive the benefit of support from her family, notwithstanding the limitation of living in different countries. The mother has had the benefit of that support (despite the geographical limitations) to date, and no evidence was advanced to suggest that such support will not continue.

DETERMINATION

In light of the above considerations, and the findings I have made, I have determined that it is in this child’s best interests that he remains living in Australia, in a location in Sydney. That outcome will afford the child the best opportunity for his relationship with the father to continue to develop and stabilise.
I acknowledge that the mother is genuine in her wish to return to live in Country E and that she will be disappointed by an outcome which sees that wish thwarted. She is the child’s primary caregiver and because of these orders, she will be compelled to provide that care in a place not of her choosing. While those considerations weigh heavily, I am persuaded that if the mother lived in Country E, the child’s important relationship with his father will be imperilled and is unlikely to be sustained. That is not in the child’s best interests. The court is tasked with weighing the advantages and disadvantages of each of the competing proposals for the child, rather than for each of the parents (McCall & Clark [2009] FamCAFC 92; (2009) FLC 93-405 at [88]) and it is the best interests of the child which remain paramount. The mother has evidenced an ability to manage her difficult circumstances while caring for the child admirably. The evidence does not lead me to expect that, apart from disappointment, the mother will decompensate in the event her relocation application is refused nor that her parenting of the child will be compromised.
As both of the child’s parents will remain living in Australia as a consequence of the orders I make, there is advantage to the child in having both of his parents involved in making long-term decisions about his care. While the mother sought that she solely exercise this decision-making power, and I identify difficulties in the co-parenting relationship, I am not persuaded that such is in the best interests of the child. The father is interested in, and attentive to, the child’s progress and development and the child will benefit from the different perspectives of his parents being applied to decision making. I also accept the evidence of the Family Report writer in that according the mother sole decision-making might serve to diminish the father’s role in the child’s life.
I am not persuaded that the parents are unable to communicate sufficiently to discharge the consultation obligations required under s 61CA of the Act. While I have found that the father did on occasion communicate disrespectfully and abusively with the mother, that conduct arose in the circumstances of the deterioration of their relationship and there has been no repeat of such unacceptable conduct since then. As recommended by the Family Report writer, communication between the parties about the child can occur via a particular communication application and I will make such an order.
The father also sought an order about day-to-day decision making. Each of the parents will be caring for the child and implicit in that role is the ability to make day to day decisions for the child, without consultation with the other parent (s 61DAB of the Act). I will make the order as sought by the father, as making express the obligations of each of the parents may assist them in navigating their future co-parenting relationship.
The child’s current time in the father’s care has been in place for more than two years and the Family Report writer had recommended a further progression to that time. The Family Report writer observed that the father’s proposal adequately managed progression of time for this child. More than two years have passed since the child commenced overnights, and he is now more than four years old. I propose to abridge the time frame for the commencement of the next phase of time, while expressing the time arrangements in such a way as to maximise the opportunity for the mother to have a full weekend of care of the child each fortnight.
The child will immediately commence having an additional night of care with the father (across a fortnight) and thereafter, I have allowed the passage of additional time before the next period of overnight care is implemented. The orders I make provide for a continuing increase (of one night each fortnight per staged progression) in the father’s care of the child until week about care commences in 2028, which ultimate living arrangement was recommended by the Family Report writer. I am satisfied that such a care arrangement will be in the best interests of this child, including because it will enable him the best opportunity to fully experience the attributes of each of his parents.
The child is expected to commence school in 2027. The Family Report writer suggested that the child would readily manage time during school holidays which was consistent with the usual spend time arrangements during term time. The Family Report writer also opined that school holiday time provided an opportunity to trial longer care periods.
The orders I make will provide for the father to care for the child for half of the term school holidays once he commences school, even though at that time, the father’s term time will be less than a week across the fortnight. School holiday time is different to term time, given the child is freed from the constraints of timetables and travel to and from school. Having a slightly longer period of time in the father’s care during holidays, in the early years of the child’s attendance at primary school, will additionally allow the child and the parents to trial these longer periods of care.
For the long school holidays commencing in the summer at the end of the child’s first year in school, I have made orders that the child spend alternating weeks with his parents, as a foundation to the commencement of week about care from the commencement of the school term in his second year of school (being Year 1). That arrangement allows some time for the child to settle into that care arrangement before the commencement of the school year.
From the year that the child commences in Year 3 at school, the long summer holiday period shall be shared equally between the parties as equal blocks (with care in the first and second halves to rotate). By this time, the child will be well settled in school and living across the two households.
The orders I propose to make will also support the mother and child travelling to Country E not less than once each year. The father proposed that prior to the child commencing primary school, such would be for up to two months in a year. That is an appropriate arrangement. Travel opportunities will be more limited once the child commences school and the father proposed in that instance, travel of up to 30 days at a time. There is no impediment to the parents reaching agreement about additional travel, and the father maintaining a generous attitude to the child spending time in Country E with his mother will no doubt be an important foundation to the future success of the co-parenting relationship, in the interests of the child.
The father proposed a scheme for notice and information to be given, prior to the proposed travel, which orders I will make. The logistics of the mother’s travel occurring this year, which will be for up to 60 days, will no doubt be easier for the parties given the child will not have started his primary schooling. In the future, the parties will need to manage between them whether and to what extent they may tolerate the child missing some school in order to facilitate the travel back to Country E with the mother, if such was not to occur only within defined school holiday periods (which order was sought by the father and which I will make).
The international travel orders, as sought by the father, will provide the mother with the opportunity to travel to Country E for a period of 30 days each year, after the child is at school. Once the child is at school, to minimise the child’s absence from school, the mother may nominate travel during the Christmas holiday period. To give effect to the order permitting the mother to have a full month of time away with the child, the father may be compelled to surrender some of his time with the child during the end of year holidays (or agree to the child not attending school for a period of time). It is not intended however, that the mother may elect to have her long summer holiday time with the child in this country and then impose upon the father, her nomination of overseas travel time to occur during his period of long summer holidays. Such an outcome would be perverse and not consistent with the shared care of the child provided in the orders I make.
The logistics of the international travel orders will require some co-operation and commonsense from the parents and the expectation of some flexibility was inherent in the orders proposed by the father. However, to provide additional boundaries, I have included an order which makes clear that the mother’s nomination of travel dates, after the child has commenced school, may only result in the surrender by the father of two weeks of school holiday time each year until the child commences Year 3 and the surrender of one week of holiday time thereafter, unless otherwise agreed. I have also made the order as proposed by the father by which he will meet the costs of the child’s travel with the mother, to Country E, until the child turns ten years old.
I have also included an order to ensure that in the event the mother travels to Country E with the child during the Australian summer, such may only include the Christmas period every second year (ensuring that the child is able to experience Christmas in Australia in the alternate year).
Neither party sought the discharge of the Family Law Watchlist Order, but such has been included in the orders I make to facilitate international travel.
Special occasion orders have been made largely in accordance with the father’s minute of order sought. If the child’s birthday falls on a changeover day between the parents, then that arrangement in itself affords each party “time” on the child’s birthday.
The father sought an order that he be able to travel internationally with the child for up to 21 days each year. That proposed order was accompanied by an order that international travel with the child by the parents be scheduled to occur as far as practicable, across school holiday periods.
In circumstances where the father’s time with the child will continue to increase gradually, the father’s international travel for such an extended period, should not occur until after the child commences Year 3. In the summer holidays of that year, the child will be having block extended time with each of his parents. Prior to that occurrence, each of the parents may elect to make shorter travel arrangements with the child, including overseas travel, within the school holiday care periods as provided in the orders.
Orders will be made about the parties maintaining current passports for the child (Australian and Country E). Those travel documents will be held by the mother when not required for travel (as sought by her). I have made that order in circumstances where the child will live predominantly with the mother until the child is in his second year of schooling and she will be travelling internationally with the child each year. While the father sought an order that he be permitted to hold the passports, no submissions were made about the basis for such.
The parties sought differing orders about the changeover of the child. Where such does not occur at daycare/school, and where the parties cannot agree, I have acceded to the location as nominated by the mother. No doubt the location the mother has nominated is presently convenient to her but where the parties are each living in rental accommodation, they may need to approach this issue in the future with sensible pragmatism.
Each party sought communication orders. In circumstances where the child will be spending time with each of the parents across each week, I have limited the frequency of the communication to two occasions each week (as suggested by the mother in her proposed minute). The parties will be required by order to keep each other advised of their addresses and contact details necessary for communication, as part of the scaffolding of their ongoing responsibilities for the care of the child.
I will include an order that requires the parents to facilitate the child communicating with the other parent at any reasonable time requested by the child.
The father sought additional orders about the management of the communication between the child and the other parent, which were not addressed in submissions. The father proposed a scheme to allow for alternate arrangements to be made in the event the child was not available at the scheduled communication time. On the one hand, that order would make express a commonsense expectation that if for any reason the child cannot be available at the ordered time, a parent should communicate with the other parent, such that alternate arrangements are made. The risk of the inclusion of such an order is that it may encourage irregularity around the contact arrangements, creating uncertainty for the child and the parties. It may also provide a framework for non-compliance with the communication order – the utility of having an express obligation is lost if that obligation may be the subject of “other arrangements”.
I have concluded that it is preferable to have a fixed framework for communication between the parties and the child. In the event a change needs to be made to those arrangements, that will be a matter for the parents.
I have included orders which make express that each parent may attend those school/day care and extracurricular activities of the child to which parents are invited or permitted to attend, as sought by the father. There are obvious advantages to the child in being able to share these occasions with his parents.
Similarly, I have included orders as sought by the father, which make express the obligation on the parents to share with the other, information about the child’s health, attendance upon doctors or medical providers and more urgent communication in the event of emergency. I am satisfied that orders about these matters will be useful for the parties and will assist in ensuring each of the parents has the necessary information in order to provide appropriate care to the child.
A range of sensible orders were sought by the father about sharing information between the parties and orders which will facilitate the parties obtaining information from third party organisations (including schools) with which the child is involved. I will make orders reflective of those.
Each of the parties sought orders for certain restraints to be imposed upon their engagement with each other and while different language was used, the matters the subject of restraint were largely consistent. The father sought an order restraining a parent from ending the child’s communication with the other parent prior to the expiration of 15 minutes from the commencement of the call and from recording the call. Such an order was not supported by evidence or submission. That order presumes an obligation for the child to continue the communication with the parent for a fixed time, which does not allow for the vagaries of communication with a young child. I decline to apply any time limit to the communication orders and decline to make the injunctions sought in paragraph 16 of the father’s minute.
I decline to make an order requiring parties to offer the other parent the first opportunity to provide care of the child if they are unable to do so (as sought by the father). No submissions were made about that order, and it may lead to conflict between the parties, arising from unwelcome scrutiny of arrangements being made by the other parent when the child is in their care.
The mother sought to limit the circumstances in which a parent might introduce the child to a new partner or a person “in a caregiving role”. I decline to make that order. There is no evidence to support making orders which encroach upon the way each of the parents order their lives and the decisions they may each make about future relationships. Those are matters to be managed by the parents and each will exercise their own judgment about those considerations.
Implicit in these orders is the requirement that the child will remain living in Australia. The father proposed that the parties be restrained from changing the child’s residence to a place outside of 30 km from Suburb B, NSW. That wide geographic allowance will permit some flexibility to the parties about their future living arrangements and will minimise the risk of future conflict about this issue. I will make that order.

Costs sought by the ICL

The ICL sought an order that the father (who is meeting the costs of his legal representation privately) should pay half of the ICL’s costs, identified as $11,459 (being half of the total contained in the ICL’s amended Costs Notice which became Exhibit 56). The mother is in receipt of a grant of legal aid.
The father has met the costs of his legal representation entirely from his own resources, including his income (with a base salary of $150,000) and by borrowing against real property that he owns.
It was submitted on behalf of the father that he would suffer financial hardship (s 114UC of the Act) in the event that he was required to pay the costs as claimed by the ICL. The father pays child support as assessed and as indicated, has offered to also contribute to the costs of the child travelling with the mother to Country E and I have made that order.
However, I am not persuaded that the financial responsibilities identified by the father leads to the conclusion that being required to contribute to the costs of the ICL will create hardship for him. Those are matters, in any event, which he may explore with representatives of Legal Aid NSW, for example, with respect to managing the timing of any payment to them.
The work of ICLs is undoubtedly important and beneficial to the parties and to the court. Where parties have the means to do so, it is appropriate that they contribute to meeting the costs of the ICL. The identified costs of the ICL are modest in comparison to the private costs incurred by litigants before this court generally and in this instance.
I will accede to the ICL’s application.
